summary:
Prostaglandin analogs have been shown through multiple studies to cause black deposits on the conjunctiva; however, other medications like beta-blockers also exhibit this effect but less frequently reported compared to prostaglandins based on current literature review findings.
To address your requirements accurately while keeping it concise within just under three lines for clarity without losing essential information regarding causative agents linked directly by medical research outcomes thus far,

Prostaglandin analogues commonly lead to black pigmentation changes noted clinically across many cases due their widespread usage among patients managing intraocular pressure issues related to open-angle glaucomas. Beta blockers occasionally present similarly albeit at lower frequency rates according recent reviews comparing different ocular hypotensive therapies' side effects profiles against each other
